title: DLL that was not present in memory despite not being formally unloaded
blurb: A Microsoft debugging post traces a third-party app's stack-overflow crashes to a recursive exception-handling death spiral that wrongly implicated shell32.dll.

title: Austria Lobbies EU to Host Anthropic After US Access Curbs
blurb: Austria is lobbying the EU to host Anthropic within the bloc, a response to US efforts to bar foreigners from accessing the lab's most advanced AI models. The push reflects growing European anxiety over dependence on US-controlled AI infrastructure and signals that export-style curbs on frontier models are reshaping where labs may need to operate.

title: An analysis of US payroll data across 730+ occupations: employment among workers ages 22 to 25 in highly AI-exposed jobs is now shrinking by 3.8% per year (Nick Lichtenberg/Fortune)
blurb: New research analyzing US payroll data across more than 730 occupations found that employment among workers ages 22 to 25 in highly AI-exposed jobs is now shrinking 3.8% per year. The decline is concentrated in early-career roles where AI tools most directly substitute for entry-level tasks, while older workers and less-exposed occupations show no comparable drop.

title: Kids act would require age checks to get online
blurb: The KIDS Act, which Congress may vote on within a week, bundles a revised KOSA with other internet bills under an expedited process that the EFF argues would pressure platforms to verify every user's age. Because liability attaches whenever a service "should have known" a user is a minor, the author contends companies will default to platform-wide age checks, eroding privacy, encryption, and anonymous browsing for adults too.

title: Data breach exposes up to 14.2 million email logins at six ISPs
blurb: KDDI Corporation disclosed a data breach that may have exposed the email addresses and passwords of up to 14.2 million current, former, and inactive customers across six Japanese ISPs, including NIFTY, BIGLOBE, and JCOM. Attackers exploited a flaw in unnamed third-party software on a shared KDDI email system, and while some passwords were hashed or encrypted, the company has not said how many were stored in plaintext.

title: EU to legislate about Chat Control behind closed doors
blurb: EU lawmakers will hold weekend negotiations on Chat Control, the proposal to mandate scanning of private messages for child sexual abuse material. Former MEP Patrick Breyer argues the process is undemocratic, citing a Friday Council push to revive an expired scanning regime the Parliament rejected in March, plus Monday trilogue talks on the permanent version that could weaken encryption and end anonymous communication.

title: Prosecutors used ChatGPT logs as evidence in the Palisades fire trial
blurb: Prosecutors in the Palisades fire arson trial entered defendant Jonathan Rinderknecht's ChatGPT logs as evidence, citing fire images he generated and prompts like asking whether someone could be blamed for a fire started by their cigarette. Jurors were unconvinced and deadlocked 10-2 for the defense, producing a mistrial, with one juror saying his chatbot use proved nothing because she talks to ChatGPT all the time.

title: Google is rationing Gemini access to Meta because it cannot provide enough compute
blurb: Google capped Meta's access to its Gemini models because it cannot meet the compute demand, the FT reported, forcing Meta to tell staff to use AI tokens more efficiently. Meta had relied on Gemini for content moderation and scam removal but is now shifting those workloads to Muse Spark, its own internal model, as Google itself rents 110,000 Nvidia GPUs from SpaceX for $920M a month.

title: California Leaders Agree to $351 Billion Budget, Software Tax
blurb: California Governor Gavin Newsom and Democratic legislative leaders agreed on a $351.7 billion state budget funded partly by a new tax on digital software sales. The software levy marks one of the first state-level attempts to tax SaaS and digital products directly, a precedent other states facing budget gaps are likely to watch closely.

title: EU Open Sources Ten-Year Network Development Planning Tools
blurb: The EU's grid operators and Open Energy Transition open-sourced Open-TYNDP, a PyPSA-based toolchain for the Ten-Year Network Development Plan that governs European electricity transmission planning. The project, a collaboration with ENTSO-E, aims to replace closed scenario-building and cost-benefit models with reproducible code that computes results from raw data, lowering the barrier for stakeholders to scrutinize multi-decade grid investments.

title: China’s Loongson launches homegrown 16-core server CPU built on LoongArch architecture — 40W chip with DDR4 ECC and 32 PCIe lanes targets cheap SMB file, database, and web servers
blurb: Loongson launched the 3C3000, a 16-core server CPU built on its homegrown LoongArch architecture, targeting low-cost file, database, and web servers for small and medium businesses. The 40W chip runs at 1.5 to 1.8 GHz with dual DDR4-2400 ECC channels and 32 PCIe lanes, and its pin-compatibility with the earlier 3B6000 lets builders reuse existing platform designs.

title: AI Startup Firmus to Build Indonesia Data Center With Nvidia
blurb: Firmus Technologies will build its first data center in Indonesia as part of a partnership with Nvidia. The Australian AI infrastructure firm expects the deal to generate as much as $30B in committed offtake agreements over its first six years.

title: Why Wall Street thinks US memory maker Micron is the next Nvidia
blurb: Micron's market cap briefly topped Meta and Tesla on Thursday, closing the week near $1.27T after the stock soared 236% in a month on AI-driven memory demand. The memory maker reported third-quarter revenue of $41.45B, up fourfold year-over-year, with profits jumping from $1.88B to $28.2B as a DRAM and NAND shortage dubbed RAMageddon is forecast to persist into 2027.

title: How Robotaxis Are Making Headway, Glitches and All
blurb: Robotaxis have moved from perpetual "next year" status to real commercial deployment, with Waymo, Tesla, Baidu, and others now running paid driverless rides in multiple cities. Persistent challenges remain, including traffic glitches, weather and edge-case handling, regulatory scrutiny, and the unresolved economics of scaling fleets profitably.

title: California will tax downloaded software for the first time as part of a $351.7 billion budget deal
blurb: California's $351.7B budget will extend sales tax to prewritten software downloaded online for the first time, a change expected to raise $2B annually starting in fiscal 2028. The deal also puts a measure on the November ballot to let the state bank more revenue from one-off windfalls like the planned OpenAI and Anthropic IPOs, hedging against federal healthcare cuts.

title: China Resets the AI Race
blurb: China Resets the AI Race argues that Chinese labs are closing the gap with US frontier models through open-weight releases and aggressive pricing rather than chasing benchmark leadership. The shift pressures American firms to defend not just model quality but the economics of inference, as cheaper Chinese alternatives erode the assumption that the most capable model wins the market.

title: Salesforce employees are confused about why the company is promoting a competitor inside Slack
blurb: Anthropic launched Claude Tag, an always-on AI agent that lives inside Slack channels and works through tasks in public view, prompting Salesforce to promote it on social media even though it competes with Salesforce's own Slackbot and Agentforce. The overlap is awkward given Salesforce paid $27.7B for Slack in 2021, but it expects to spend $300M on Anthropic tokens this year and holds a roughly 1% stake in the $380B company.

title: Apple’s Sweeping Price Increases Bring Home the Costs of the AI Era
blurb: Apple is raising prices across its product lineup, including the upcoming M6 and M7 chips and a touchscreen MacBook Pro, citing the rising cost of building AI capabilities into its devices. The increases pass the expense of the AI buildout directly to consumers, marking a shift after years of Apple holding flagship prices steady to protect unit sales.

title: OpenAI poaches Apple Vision Pro and smart glasses chief
blurb: OpenAI hired Paul Meade, Apple's vice president in charge of Vision Pro hardware engineering and its smart glasses program, to work on its upcoming family of AI-powered devices. Meade led the Vision Products Group for seven years and is the latest senior Apple hardware leader to join OpenAI's hardware unit, which already includes Jony Ive and former design chief Evans Hankey.

title: PlayStation is removing over 500 movies from UK customers' accounts with no refunds — Iconic films like Terminator 2, Apocalypse Now, and Mulholland Drive are getting deleted
blurb: Sony will delete 551 StudioCanal movies, including Terminator 2, Apocalypse Now, and Mulholland Drive, from UK PlayStation customers' accounts on September 1 with no refunds, citing expired licensing agreements. The purge underscores that "buying" digital media grants only a revocable license, leaving paid-for libraries at the mercy of whatever contracts a platform declines to renew.

title: The Taser CEO Who Says AI Is the Future of Policing
blurb: Axon CEO Rick Smith is staking the company's future on AI-driven policing tools, expanding beyond the Tasers and body cameras that built its market dominance. Smith's vision, tied to his own performance-based pay package, pushes Axon toward software like automated report writing and real-time video analysis as the next growth engine.

title: Flock cameras track more than your license plate, and they're spreading fast
blurb: Flock Safety's AI surveillance cameras have spread to over 100,000 installations across the US, logging vehicle movements and letting police run natural-language searches like "green sedan with a flag bumper sticker" even without a license plate. The author argues the "license plate reader" label undersells the threat, citing documented security flaws, law-enforcement misuse, and AI errors that have landed innocent people in legal trouble.

title: An analysis of US payroll data across 730+ occupations: employment among workers ages 22 to 25 in highly AI-exposed jobs is now shrinking by 3.8% per year (Nick Lichtenberg/Fortune)
blurb: A Fortune analysis of US payroll data across more than 730 occupations found that employment among workers ages 22 to 25 in highly AI-exposed jobs is now shrinking by 3.8% per year. The decline is concentrated in entry-level roles most exposed to automation, while older workers and less-exposed occupations have not seen the same contraction.

title: OpenAI Codex lead on the new shape of product work | Andrew Ambrosino
blurb: In this conversation with Lenny's Newsletter, OpenAI Codex desktop lead Andrew Ambrosino argues that AI has inverted the product development process and that "taste" is becoming the most valuable professional skill in an AI-first workplace. He notes that nearly 100% of OpenAI employees, not just engineers, now use Codex weekly, and describes a "zone defense" model for product managers when anyone on a team can build anything.

title: Prosecutors used ChatGPT logs as evidence in the Palisades fire trial
blurb: Prosecutors in the Palisades fire arson trial entered Jonathan Rinderknecht's ChatGPT logs as evidence, citing fire images he generated and prompts asking whether someone could be blamed for a cigarette-lit fire. Jurors were unconvinced, voting 10-2 for the defense and forcing a mistrial, with one juror saying his chatbot use proved nothing because she talks to ChatGPT all the time.

title: Wayfinder Router: deterministic routing of queries between local and hosted LLM
blurb: Wayfinder Router is a deterministic prompt-complexity router that decides whether to send each query to a local or hosted LLM by reading the prompt's structure and wording, offline and in microseconds, without calling another model to make the call. It composes with provider gateways like OpenRouter or LiteLLM, handling the cheap-versus-expensive tier decision while they handle which provider serves the call.

title: Google is rationing Gemini access to Meta because it cannot provide enough compute
blurb: Google capped Meta's access to its Gemini models because it cannot supply the compute capacity Meta wanted, the Financial Times reported, prompting Meta to tell staff to use AI tokens more efficiently. Meta had relied on Gemini over its own Llama models for safety tasks like content moderation, but is now shifting those workloads to Muse Spark, a new internal model under its Superintelligence Labs division.

title: Austria Lobbies EU to Host Anthropic After US Access Curbs
blurb: Austria is lobbying the European Union to host Anthropic within the bloc, a response to US efforts to block foreigners from accessing its most advanced AI models. The proposal would position the EU as a jurisdiction for frontier model access as Washington tightens export controls on US-developed systems.

title: The BIS warns an AI bust could hit credit markets as hard as the 2008 financial crisis
blurb: The Bank for International Settlements warned in its annual report that an AI investment bust could disrupt credit markets as severely as the 2008 financial crisis. The central banks' central bank singled out "circular financing," where chipmakers take equity stakes in AI labs that then commit to buying chips back, as a vulnerability that leaves poorly disclosed risk and the same asset pledged multiple times.

title: AI Startup Firmus to Build Indonesia Data Center With Nvidia
blurb: Firmus Technologies will build its first data center in Indonesia through a partnership with Nvidia, marking the Australian AI infrastructure firm's initial project in the country. The company said the deal is expected to generate as much as $30B in committed offtake agreements over its first six years.

title: OpenAI poaches Apple Vision Pro and smart glasses chief
blurb: OpenAI hired Paul Meade, the Apple vice president who led Vision Pro hardware engineering for seven years and oversaw the company's smart glasses effort, to work on its upcoming family of AI-powered devices. The move extends OpenAI's recruitment of Apple hardware talent into io, the Jony Ive subsidiary building AI-first devices, and follows a hardware reshuffle under incoming CEO John Ternus.

title: China Resets the AI Race
blurb: China is reshaping the global AI race through state-backed investment, open-weight model releases, and domestic chip development that narrow the gap with US labs. The shift pressures American firms that had assumed a durable lead, as Chinese models increasingly compete on capability while undercutting Western pricing and spreading through open distribution.

title: An analysis of US payroll data across 730+ occupations: employment among workers ages 22 to 25 in highly AI-exposed jobs is now shrinking by 3.8% per year (Nick Lichtenberg/Fortune)
blurb: Entry-level hiring is where AI is showing up first in the labor data. An analysis of US payroll records across more than 730 occupations found that employment among workers ages 22 to 25 in highly AI-exposed jobs is now shrinking by 3.8% per year, even as older and less-exposed cohorts hold steady. The squeeze lands on the cheapest, most automatable rung of the workforce, which is also the one founders have always leaned on to staff junior roles cheaply.

title: Austria Lobbies EU to Host Anthropic After US Access Curbs
blurb: Austria is lobbying the EU to host Anthropic inside its borders, a direct response to US moves to block foreigners from accessing the most advanced AI models. The pitch reframes frontier AI as sovereign infrastructure, something a country wants physically located where its own firms can rely on uninterrupted access. For founders building on US labs, it signals that model access is becoming a geopolitical variable, and where your provider sits could soon determine what you can ship and to whom.

title: Kids act would require age checks to get online
blurb: Congress is preparing to vote within the week on the KIDS Act, a bundled package built around a revised KOSA that pressures online services to verify the age of every user. The bill never mandates age checks outright, but it imposes liability whenever a platform "knows or should have known" a user is under 16, a negligence standard that makes blanket age-gating the safest legal option. For founders building anything consumer-facing, the practical effect is a compliance burden that lands on the entire user base, not just minors, and adds real legal risk to how you handle identity and private messaging.

title: California Leaders Agree to $351 Billion Budget, Software Tax
blurb: California's $351.7 billion budget leans on a new tax on digital software sales to balance the books. The state is reaching into the SaaS economy precisely because that is where the money now sits, and a tax on software sales eventually flows to whoever buys the software. Any founder selling into California should expect the cost to surface somewhere, whether vendors raise prices or buyers tighten budgets to absorb it.

title: The BIS warns an AI bust could hit credit markets as hard as the 2008 financial crisis
blurb: The BIS, the central bank for central banks, warned in its annual report that an AI investment bust could disrupt credit markets as severely as the 2008 financial crisis. The specific vulnerability it named is circular financing, where chipmakers take equity stakes in AI labs that then commit to buying those same chips, with deal terms so poorly disclosed that the same asset can be pledged multiple times. If returns disappoint and financing pulls back, the capex boom flips into a protracted bust, and the leverage hiding inside these arrangements becomes everyone's problem.

title: California will tax downloaded software for the first time as part of a $351.7 billion budget deal
blurb: California's new $351.7B budget extends sales tax to downloaded software for the first time, a change expected to raise about $2B a year, $900M for the state and $1.1B for local governments, starting in fiscal 2028. The same deal puts a measure on the November ballot to let the state bank more of the windfall from AI IPOs like OpenAI's and Anthropic's, since its progressive tax base swings hard with the market. For any software business operating in California, a line item that was untaxed for years is about to carry sales tax.

title: Google is rationing Gemini access to Meta because it cannot provide enough compute
blurb: Google capped Meta's access to Gemini because it cannot supply the compute Meta wanted, pushing Meta to ration AI tokens internally and accelerate its own Muse Spark model. The signal is stark: Google is spending over $180B on capex this year and still rents 110,000 Nvidia GPUs from SpaceX for $920M a month, yet cannot serve a customer as large as Meta. When the company with one of the world's biggest infrastructure pools is rationing access, the compute shortage is no longer a constraint on startups alone but a structural limit reshaping deals between the largest players.

title: Why Wall Street thinks US memory maker Micron is the next Nvidia
blurb: Micron's stock has soared over 236% in the past month, briefly pushing its market cap past Meta and Tesla before settling near $1.27 trillion on Friday. The AI data center buildout has created a shortage of DRAM, NAND, and especially High-Bandwidth Memory, dubbed RAMageddon, that is forecast to persist into 2027. Third-quarter revenue quadrupled year-over-year to $41.45 billion as profits jumped from $1.88 billion to $28.2 billion, and Wall Street, hunting for the next Nvidia, is betting the memory crunch lasts long enough to justify the run.

title: The Taser CEO Who Says AI Is the Future of Policing
blurb: Axon CEO Rick Smith is betting the company's market dominance, and his own pay package, on AI becoming the core of modern policing. The maker of Tasers and body cameras already supplies a large share of US police departments, and Smith is pushing tools that automate report-writing, evidence review, and real-time analysis on top of that installed base. The wager is that owning the hardware cops already wear becomes the distribution channel for selling them recurring AI software, turning a gear vendor into a policing-software platform.

title: OpenAI Codex lead on the new shape of product work | Andrew Ambrosino
blurb: When everyone on your team can build anything, the bottleneck stops being who can ship and becomes who has the taste to know what's worth shipping. Codex's desktop lead at OpenAI argues AI has inverted product development, collapsing the old engineer-designer-PM roles into a single build function while making judgment the scarce skill. His "zone defense" model for PMs is the practical answer: when anyone can produce the work, the job is owning a problem area and steering it, not guarding a discipline. Nearly 100% of OpenAI employees, not just engineers, now use Codex weekly.

title: Apple’s Sweeping Price Increases Bring Home the Costs of the AI Era
blurb: Apple is raising prices across its product line, and the reason is the AI buildout it can no longer absorb quietly. New silicon, on-device inference, and the compute capex behind it are landing on the consumer, signaling that the AI era's costs have moved from cloud balance sheets to hardware sticker prices. For founders, it is a marker that the "AI is basically free" phase is ending, and the companies that priced on subsidized economics now have to explain why their product is worth what it actually costs to run.

title: Wayfinder Router: deterministic routing of queries between local and hosted LLM
blurb: Wayfinder Router scores a prompt's structure (length, headings, lists, code) and wording to decide whether it goes to your small local model or a big cloud one, in microseconds and fully offline with no API key, network call, or model judge. Unlike classifier- or LLM-based routers, the decision is deterministic and free, and it composes with gateways like LiteLLM or OpenRouter, which still pick the provider underneath.

title: WAL-RUS: a Rust Rewrite of WAL-G for PostgreSQL Backups
blurb: ClickHouse released WAL-RUS, an open-source Rust reimplementation of WAL-G for PostgreSQL WAL archival and backups, designed to be WAL-G compatible while delivering a predictable memory profile. The motivation is Go's garbage-collected runtime, whose sawtooth memory pattern and reserved virtual memory force operators to over-provision backup infrastructure, taking RAM away from Postgres queries, shared buffers, and page cache.

title: DLL that was not present in memory despite not being formally unloaded
blurb: A debugging post-mortem traces a third-party program's crashes, initially blamed on shell32.dll, to a recursive exception-handling death spiral where an exception thrown inside RtlLookupFunctionEntry restarts KiUserExceptionDispatch on every pass until the stack overflows. Walking to the bottom of the call stack shows shell32 was only fingered as the apparent origin, and the post explains why the real fault sits in the exception dispatch loop rather than the library it was assigned to.

title: WAL-RUS: a Rust Rewrite of WAL-G for PostgreSQL Backups
blurb: ClickHouse released WAL-RUS, an open-source Rust rewrite of WAL-G that handles Postgres backups and WAL archival while staying compatible with WAL-G's format. The goal is a predictable memory profile: Go's garbage-collected runtime produces a sawtooth memory pattern that forces operators to over-provision, leaving less headroom for Postgres queries, shared buffers, and page cache in resource-constrained environments.

title: Instagram wants to make algorithm customisation a core part of the app, not a buried setting
blurb: Instagram is testing ways to surface Your Algorithm, its topic-tuning tool, directly inside the feed through pull-down gestures, swipe-up prompts on Reels, and per-Reel buttons for more or less of similar content. Head Adam Mosseri framed the shift as moving the feature from a buried setting to something central, even as top comments on his post kept asking for a simple chronological feed of accounts they already follow.

title: David Kohn Architects designs London penthouse to resemble "house on a hill"
blurb: David Kohn Architects completed House on a Hill, a three-storey penthouse atop a Victorian warehouse in Covent Garden, treating the rooftop as a new ground plane with stepped bay windows and a planted terrace. The southern living spaces (dining room, library, and lounge) run as a doorless enfilade opening onto the garden, framing central London skyline views from a retreat positioned at the city's center.

title: What are hypertokens? The layer between tokens and components, rebuilt for agents
blurb: Jake Albaugh of Figma coined "hypertokens" at Config 2026 to name the layer between single-decision tokens and full components: a bundle of related decisions like a heading's family, size, weight, and line height, defined once so machines read intent instead of guessing it. The argument is that AI speeds execution but not clarity, so design-system drift that humans once smoothed over by eye now compounds when agents build exactly what they find.

title: Flock cameras track more than your license plate, and they're spreading fast
blurb: Flock Safety's automated license plate readers have surpassed 100,000 installations across the US, and they capture far more than plates, supporting natural-language searches for vehicles by color, bumper stickers, and damage. The systems run modified Android, transmit footage to AI-cataloged databases accessible to law enforcement, and have drawn scrutiny over security vulnerabilities, officer misuse, and AI misidentifications that have ensnared innocent people.

title: Data breach exposes up to 14.2 million email logins at six ISPs
blurb: KDDI Corporation disclosed a breach of an email system shared across six Japanese ISPs (including JCOM, NIFTY, and BIGLOBE) after attackers exploited a vulnerability in unnamed third-party software, exposing up to 14.2 million customer email addresses and passwords. KDDI discovered the compromise on June 17 and blocked the attacker; some passwords were hashed or encrypted, but the company has not specified how many were stored in plaintext, so affected customers should reset passwords and enable 2FA immediately.

title: EU to legislate about Chat Control behind closed doors
blurb: The EU Council is meeting Friday to adopt a first-reading position reviving the expired Chat Control 1.0 mass-scanning regime, while final trilogue negotiations on the permanent CSAR regulation (2022/0155) proceed Monday, June 29. Critics including former MEP Patrick Breyer warn the parallel moves would mandate client-side scanning of encrypted messages and end anonymous communication, bypassing the Parliament's March rejection of the proposal.

title: Kids act would require age checks to get online
blurb: Congress is preparing to vote within the next week on the KIDS Act, a package combining a revised Kids Online Safety Act with other internet bills under an expedited process. The EFF argues that despite a disclaimer that KOSA doesn't mandate age verification, its "knows or should have known" negligence standard will push platforms toward verifying every user's age, undermining privacy and encrypted communications.

title: California will tax downloaded software for the first time as part of a $351.7 billion budget deal
blurb: California's $351.7B budget deal extends sales tax to prewritten software downloaded from the web for the first time, a change expected to raise $900M for the state and $1.1B for local governments annually starting in fiscal 2028. Enterprise IT buyers in California face higher costs on everything from office productivity suites to electronic medical records, and the precedent could prompt other states to tax SaaS and downloaded software similarly.

title: Data breach exposes up to 14.2 million email logins at six ISPs
blurb: KDDI Corporation disclosed a breach of a shared email system that may have exposed the addresses and passwords of up to 14.2 million current, former, and inactive customers across six Japanese ISPs, including JCOM, NIFTY, and BIGLOBE. Attackers exploited a vulnerability in unnamed third-party software, and while some passwords were hashed or encrypted, KDDI did not disclose how many were stored in plaintext.

title: Salesforce employees are confused about why the company is promoting a competitor inside Slack
blurb: Anthropic launched Claude Tag, an always-on AI agent that works inside Slack channels, and Salesforce promoted it publicly even though it competes directly with Salesforce's own Slackbot and Agentforce. The overlap creates a positioning problem for enterprise buyers now choosing among three AI interfaces in one workspace, but Salesforce has financial reasons to stay model-agnostic, expecting to spend $300M on Anthropic tokens this year and holding a roughly 1% stake in the $380B company.

title: WAL-RUS: a Rust Rewrite of WAL-G for PostgreSQL Backups
blurb: ClickHouse released WAL-RUS, an open-source Rust rewrite of WAL-G that handles Postgres WAL archival and backups while keeping WAL-G compatibility. The rewrite targets memory predictability, replacing Go's garbage-collected sawtooth footprint with a tighter profile so operators can stop over-provisioning backup memory and hand it back to Postgres for shared buffers and page cache.

title: Instagram wants to make algorithm customisation a core part of the app, not a buried setting
blurb: Instagram is testing new ways to surface Your Algorithm, the topic-tuning tool that lets users dial recommendations up or down, through pull-down feed gestures, swipe-up Reel prompts, and per-Reel more/less buttons. The most-liked comments on Mosseri's post asked for the opposite, a chronological feed of accounts they follow, underscoring the unresolved gap between Meta's discovery-first algorithm and what users say they want.

title: OpenAI Codex lead on the new shape of product work | Andrew Ambrosino
blurb: In this interview, OpenAI's Codex desktop app lead Andrew Ambrosino argues that AI has inverted the product development process and made "taste" the most valuable PM skill, since anyone on the team can now build almost anything. He describes a "zone defense" model where PMs at OpenAI cover overlapping territory rather than owning fixed roles, and notes that nearly all OpenAI employees now use Codex weekly. The practical takeaway for PMs: as building gets cheap, judgment about what to build becomes the differentiator.

title: Instagram wants to make algorithm customisation a core part of the app, not a buried setting
blurb: Instagram is testing ways to surface Your Algorithm, its topic-tuning controls, through in-feed pull-down gestures and per-Reel buttons rather than a buried settings menu, moving customization into the scroll itself. The move is a bet that discoverability drives adoption, though top comments on Mosseri's post show many users still want the simpler thing Instagram isn't building: a chronological feed of accounts they follow.

title: SBI's $289 million Bitbank deal is symptomatic of Japan's crypto consolidation: Architect Partners
blurb: SBI Holdings agreed to acquire Japanese exchange Bitbank for $289 million, doubling its crypto assets under custody to roughly 1.1 trillion yen and adding nearly 1 million accounts as tougher rules push the country's market toward consolidation. Architect Partners called it a bet on regulated scale over earnings, naming privately held bitFlyer as the likely next target as standalone exchanges face rising compliance costs.

title: The BIS warns an AI bust could hit credit markets as hard as the 2008 financial crisis
blurb: The BIS warned in its annual report that an AI investment bust could disrupt credit markets as severely as the 2008 financial crisis, ranking it alongside inflation and fiscal stress as a pressure point. The Basel institution flagged "circular financing," where chipmakers and hyperscalers take equity stakes in AI labs that then commit to buying their chips, creating poorly disclosed risk and assets pledged multiple times.

title: SBI's $289 million Bitbank deal is symptomatic of Japan's crypto consolidation: Architect Partners
blurb: SBI Holdings agreed to acquire Japanese exchange Bitbank for $289 million, doubling its crypto assets under custody to roughly 1.1 trillion yen and adding nearly 1 million accounts as tougher Japanese rules raise the cost of running standalone exchanges. Architect Partners reads the deal as a bet on regulated scale over earnings, and expects consolidation to continue, with private-equity-owned bitFlyer the obvious next target.

title: California Leaders Agree to $351 Billion Budget, Software Tax
blurb: California lawmakers and Governor Newsom agreed on a $351.7B state budget funded in part by a newly proposed tax on digital software sales. The levy would extend sales tax to software purchased by businesses, raising costs for fintech and SaaS operators headquartered in the state and setting a precedent other revenue-hungry states could follow.
